news_summary: Today’s environmental news highlights progress in clean energy planning, recovery of damaged seabeds, increased ancient woodland areas, and the successful rescue of a vital ocean research program.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les progrès réalisés en matière de planification des énergies propres, la restauration des fonds marins endommagés, l'extension des zones de forêts anciennes et le sauvetage d'un programme de recherche océanographique essentiel.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan los avances en la planificación de las energías limpias, la recuperación de los fondos marinos dañados, el aumento de las superficies de bosques antiguos y el rescate con éxito de un programa de investigación oceánica de vital importancia.

articles.title: Climate crisis: UN chief lays out solutions blueprint for clean energy transition
articles.summary: As a deadly heatwave continued to grip Europe on Tuesday, UN Secretary-General António Guterres issued an impassioned appeal for more ambitious global action on climate change caused by fossil fuels, to prevent irreversible damage.
articles.content:
In a major keynote speech at London Climate Action Week, the UN chief called on AI firms to "come clean" on the full environmental impact of data centres in terms their carbon, water and land footprints.

The Secretary-General also highlighted how the world’s dependence on oil is driving both the climate crisis and an energy sovereignty crunch, the latter linked to massive shipping disruption in the Strait of Hormuz and the war involving Iran, Israel and the United States.

Tweet URL

“These crises may seem separate but they share the same destructive origin: fossil fuels. And they demand the same answer: a fast, fair transition to clean energy and a surge in adaptation, resilience and climate justice for those already facing climate harm,” Mr. Guterres said, in a call for political leadership to push through global change akin to that required to phase out leaded gasoline and to ban chemicals that created a hole in the ozone layer.

In brief: the UN plan for energy independence

Cut emissions fast: emissions must peak now and reach net zero by 2050, including through a global push to curb methane pollution.

emissions must peak now and reach net zero by 2050, including through a global push to curb methane pollution. Accelerate clean energy: renewables pick-up needs to continue, subsidies must end for fossil fuel projects and fossil fuel profits taxed to support vulnerable communities and the energy transition.

renewables pick-up needs to continue, subsidies must end for fossil fuel projects and fossil fuel profits taxed to support vulnerable communities and the energy transition. Clean up AI: require major AI companies to disclose the environmental impact of their data centres and power them with renewable energy by 2030.

require major AI companies to disclose the environmental impact of their data centres and power them with renewable energy by 2030. Ensure a just transition: ensure the shift to clean energy creates jobs, supports communities and delivers development benefits for developing countries.

ensure the shift to clean energy creates jobs, supports communities and delivers development benefits for developing countries. Boost climate resilience: increase investment in adaptation, early warning systems and other measures to protect people most vulnerable to climate impacts.

increase investment in adaptation, early warning systems and other measures to protect people most vulnerable to climate impacts. Unlock fair finance: expand affordable funding for developing countries to invest in clean energy, climate adaptation and sustainable development.

expand affordable funding for developing countries to invest in clean energy, climate adaptation and sustainable development. Defend science and truth: strengthen trust in science, combat climate disinformation and protect environmental journalists and human rights defenders.

Earth’s tipping points

It is more than a decade since world leaders agreed in Paris to limit global temperature rise to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels, a remarkable show of international unity, led by the UN. Today, although that Agreement stands – and despite the US officially withdrawing for a second time in January this year – UN-backed scientists warn that average annual temperatures are likely to exceed that threshold in coming years.

“Every fraction of a degree matters,” the Secretary-General insisted, as he forewarned of the irreversible damage to coral reefs unable to survive in too-warm waters, the melting ice sheets that threaten to reshape coastlines and displace millions, and the real possibility that some small island nations could disappear under the waves.

Faced with this existential scenario, “the task before us is to strictly limit the overshoot, shorten its duration and bring temperatures down below 1.5°C as fast as possible”, Mr. Guterres maintained.

‘Mother of all energy shocks’

And while he pointed out that “any peace agreement is welcome and would bring much needed relief”, in reference to a 60-day pause in hostilities to allow ongoing Iranian-US talks in Switzerland, the UN chief noted that the Middle East crisis had unleashed “the mother of all energy shocks” comparable to the oil disruption of the 1970s and the full-scale Russian invasion of Ukraine.

As damaging as the Middle East war has been for highly industrialized nations, the UN Secretary-General insisted that developing countries have been hit even harder:

“It is a debt shock, a food shock, a development shock”, he told the London audience.

A just future from renewables

“The good news is – unlike every past energy crisis – we now have a clear way out, a clean way out,” the Secretary-General continued.

He noted that since 2010, the cost of solar energy has plummeted by almost 90 per cent, onshore wind by more than 70 per cent, and battery storage by 95 per cent.

Renewables avoided more than the annual carbon dioxide emissions of the US, the EU and Japan combined, Mr. Guterres said, adding that clean energy investment now attracts almost twice as much as fossil fuels.

“There are no embargoes on sunlight and no blockades on the wind,” he said.

A seven-point plan for energy independence

As part of the Secretary-General’s blueprint for a clean break with fossil fuels, he outlined seven key steps:

1: Emissions must peak immediately and fall steeply this decade, reaching net zero by 2050. The G20 group of wealthy nations “must lead” on this, as it is responsible for around 80 per cent of global emissions, Mr. Guterres said. Ambitious measures include a global Call to Action on Methane to reduce emissions of the gas traps around 80 times more heat than carbon dioxide, but which breaks down in the atmosphere within just a decade or two.

“The world phased out leaded gasoline. We eliminated ozone-depleting chemicals. Methane pollution must be next,” the UN chief stressed.

2: Clean energy projects should be promoted and public subsidies ditched for new fossil fuel projects. “The eight largest fossil fuel companies reported pocketing an extra $6.5 billion in the first quarter of this year alone…I urge governments to tax them" to help vulnerable families and communities and accelerate the shift to clean, affordable energy, Mr. Guterres said.

3: Every major AI company should “measure and publicly disclose the full environmental impact” of data centres: their carbon, water and land footprints – and commit to power every data centre with renewable energy by 2030. Today, AI data centres already consume more electricity than most nations; “it’s time to come clean”, the UN chief noted.

By 2030, AI data centres could use enough water to meet the basic needs of all 1.3 billion residents of sub‑Saharan Africa for an entire year, the UN chief said.

4: “No more extraction without development:” Mr. Guterres called for greater support for the move to clean energy in a way that benefits workers and communities everywhere and developing countries too, driven forward by the UN Climate Conference – COP31 – in Türkiye. “The transition itself is no longer in question,” he stressed, adding: “It will be either managed or chaotic, fair or unequal, a source of stability or of greater division; and these choices are still ours to make.”

5: Protect those most at risk from climate chaos by helping them adapt, because this “saves lives, safeguards homes and communities, helps economies absorb shocks and holds societies together”, the Secretary-General insisted. Contingency systems need to be put in place before shocks become humanitarian and economic catastrophes, Mr. Guterres added. At the same time, developed countries must deliver on their “long-standing commitment to double adaptation finance, with a clear trajectory toward tripling it”, he said.

6: Support fair finance to support phasing out fossil fuels and the green transition at scale and at speed: because many developing countries face borrowing costs that are two to three times higher than in wealthier economies.

“Countries rich in renewable potential are being locked out of the clean energy revolution,” the UN chief maintained, pointing to African countries which receive only two per cent of global clean energy investment even though they possess 60 per cent of the world’s best solar resources.

Mr. Guterres highlighted the $600-800 billion in additional lending capacity of multilateral development banks, such as the World Bank. This should be used “aggressively” to finance the infrastructure of the future and climate adaptation, along with other investment measures such as taxing high-emitting sectors, he maintained.

Equally, “developed countries must keep their promises", including support to the Fund for Responding to Loss and Damage and the Green Climate Fund, the Secretary-General continued, noting that the $300 billion pledged to developing countries must be delivered along with concrete steps to mobilize $1.3 trillion a year by 2035.

7: Finally, the UN Secretary-General urged support for science as the bedrock of truth and early warning systems - and to tackle climate falsehoods, since "disinformation is spreading deliberately to delay climate action, entrench vested interests, and erode trust”.

Tweet URL

Human rights defenders and journalists reporting on the climate and the environment should be protected and trust in evidence and institutions bolstered, Mr. Guterres insisted, pointing to the Global Initiative for Information Integrity on Climate Change, led by the UN, UNESCO and Brazil in support of this goal.

A warning from the trees of Kew

Receiving the Kew International Medal later in the day, the Secretary-General paid tribute to the Royal Botanic Gardens as a global centre of science and conservation, while warning that the climate crisis is already taking a toll on the natural world. Citing the loss of more than 400 trees at Kew during the 2022 drought and the growing risks facing many of its species, he said the fate of nature and humanity are inseparable.

The UN chief stressed that ending dependence on fossil fuels and accelerating the shift to renewable energy must go hand in hand with protecting forests, restoring degraded ecosystems, safeguarding oceans and defending science. “When the climate crisis comes for the great trees of Kew, it is a warning to us all,” he said, urging greater investment in nature-based solutions to help secure a safer and more sustainable future for people and planet alike.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant and detailed blueprint from the UN Secretary-General for a global clean energy transition, addressing climate change with concrete, large-scale actions and policies. It highlights measurable benefits such as emissions reduction, renewable energy acceleration, climate resilience, and fair finance for developing countries, all of which have broad societal and environmental impact. The article is focused, substantive, and presents an inspiring roadmap for tackling the climate crisis.
articles.personality_title: UN chief presents detailed plan for fast clean energy transition
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The world faces a climate crisis caused mainly by burning fossil fuels like oil and coal. Temperatures are rising, causing heatwaves, melting ice, and threats to nature and people. The UN Secretary-General António Guterres spoke during London Climate Action Week about urgent global action needed to stop further damage.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, Mr. Guterres outlined a seven-point plan to move quickly from fossil fuels to clean energy. His plan includes cutting emissions now and reaching net zero by 2050, ending fossil fuel subsidies, requiring AI companies to reveal and reduce their data centers’ environmental impact, supporting workers and developing countries, investing in climate adaptation, expanding fair financing, and protecting science and truth.

**Impact** – This plan is important because it offers clear, practical steps to reduce pollution and protect communities most affected by climate change. For example, solar and wind energy costs have dropped dramatically, making clean energy more affordable than ever. The plan also highlights how some countries with the best renewable resources, like many in Africa, currently receive very little investment. By addressing fairness and science, the plan aims to make the energy shift both effective and just.

**What's next step** – The UN hopes countries, especially wealthy ones that produce most emissions, will lead by acting on these steps quickly. The upcoming UN Climate Conference (COP31) in Türkiye will be a key moment to push for these changes. Governments will need to stop funding fossil fuels, tax big polluters, support vulnerable communities, and increase money for clean energy projects and climate protection.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN Secretary-General’s clear seven-step plan offers a practical path for the world to switch to clean energy, reduce pollution, and protect people and nature from climate harm.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le chef de l’ONU présente un plan détaillé pour une transition rapide vers l’énergie propre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le monde fait face à une crise climatique causée principalement par la combustion des énergies fossiles comme le pétrole et le charbon. Les températures augmentent, provoquant des vagues de chaleur, la fonte des glaces et des menaces pour la nature et les populations. Le Secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a pris la parole lors de la Semaine d’action pour le climat à Londres pour appeler à une action mondiale urgente afin d’éviter de nouveaux dégâts.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, M. Guterres a présenté un plan en sept points pour passer rapidement des énergies fossiles aux énergies propres. Son plan inclut de réduire les émissions dès maintenant pour atteindre la neutralité carbone en 2050, de supprimer les subventions aux énergies fossiles, d’exiger des entreprises d’intelligence artificielle qu’elles révèlent et réduisent l’impact environnemental de leurs centres de données, de soutenir les travailleurs et les pays en développement, d’investir dans l’adaptation au climat, d’étendre le financement équitable et de protéger la science et la vérité.

**Impact** – Ce plan est important car il propose des mesures claires et pratiques pour réduire la pollution et protéger les communautés les plus touchées par le changement climatique. Par exemple, le coût du solaire et de l’éolien a fortement baissé, rendant les énergies propres plus abordables que jamais. Le plan souligne aussi que certains pays, comme beaucoup en Afrique qui ont un fort potentiel renouvelable, reçoivent très peu d’investissements. En abordant les questions de justice et de science, le plan vise à rendre la transition énergétique efficace et équitable.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU espère que les pays, surtout les plus riches responsables de la majorité des émissions, agiront rapidement selon ces mesures. La prochaine Conférence sur le climat de l’ONU (COP31) en Turquie sera un moment clé pour faire avancer ces changements. Les gouvernements devront cesser de financer les énergies fossiles, taxer les gros pollueurs, soutenir les communautés vulnérables et augmenter les fonds pour les projets d’énergie propre et la protection climatique.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le plan clair en sept étapes du Secrétaire général de l’ONU offre une voie concrète pour que le monde passe à l’énergie propre, réduise la pollution et protège les populations et la nature des effets du climat.
articles.personality_title_es: El jefe de la ONU presenta un plan detallado para una rápida transición a la energía limpia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El mundo enfrenta una crisis climática causada principalmente por la quema de combustibles fósiles como el petróleo y el carbón. Las temperaturas suben, provocando olas de calor, derretimiento de hielos y amenazas para la naturaleza y las personas. El Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, habló durante la Semana de Acción Climática en Londres sobre la necesidad urgente de una acción global para evitar más daños.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, Guterres presentó un plan de siete puntos para pasar rápidamente de los combustibles fósiles a la energía limpia. Su plan incluye reducir las emisiones ya y alcanzar cero emisiones netas para 2050, acabar con los subsidios a los combustibles fósiles, exigir a las empresas de inteligencia artificial que revelen y reduzcan el impacto ambiental de sus centros de datos, apoyar a los trabajadores y países en desarrollo, invertir en adaptación climática, ampliar la financiación justa y proteger la ciencia y la verdad.

**Impacto** – Este plan es importante porque ofrece pasos claros y prácticos para reducir la contaminación y proteger a las comunidades más afectadas por el cambio climático. Por ejemplo, el costo de la energía solar y eólica ha bajado mucho, haciendo la energía limpia más accesible que nunca. El plan también muestra que algunos países con grandes recursos renovables, como muchos en África, reciben poca inversión. Al incluir la justicia y la ciencia, el plan busca que la transición energética sea efectiva y justa.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU espera que los países, especialmente los ricos que producen la mayoría de las emisiones, lideren actuando rápido según estos pasos. La próxima Conferencia Climática de la ONU (COP31) en Turquía será un momento clave para impulsar estos cambios. Los gobiernos deberán dejar de financiar combustibles fósiles, gravar a los grandes contaminadores, apoyar a las comunidades vulnerables y aumentar el dinero para proyectos de energía limpia y protección climática.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El plan claro de siete pasos del Secretario General de la ONU ofrece una ruta práctica para que el mundo cambie a energía limpia, reduzca la contaminación y proteja a las personas y la naturaleza del daño climático.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant globe transformed into a lush, green energy hub where stylized solar panels and wind turbines gently emerge from the earth, surrounded by glowing data center icons powered by swirling streams of clean water and light, with a protective circle of diverse hands and roots symbolizing global unity, justice, and resilience in the clean energy transition.

articles.title: Seabed damaged by fishing showing signs of recovery
articles.summary: Campaigners say improvements to the area trashed by illegal dredging show that marine protection works.
articles.content:
Early signs of recovery have been found on an area of protected seabed which was destroyed by illegal fishing in the Summer Isles near Ullapool .

Conservationists say it shows the marine ecosystem is able to repair itself if properly shielded from trawlers and dredgers which drag their gear along the seabed to scoop up catch.

The Our Seas coalition of scientists, activists and some fishermen accuse ministers of delaying action to protect at least 30% of Scotland's inshore waters, and are preparing to present a petition at the Scottish parliament.

Fishing leaders say banning some boats is too "simplistic" while the Scottish government says it takes protecting the marine environment seriously.
articles.reasoning: The article reports early signs of recovery in a protected seabed area previously damaged by illegal fishing, demonstrating that marine protection measures can lead to ecosystem restoration. This has broad environmental significance and highlights the positive impact of conservation efforts.
articles.personality_title: Protected seabed shows early signs of healing after illegal fishing damage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The seabed near the Summer Isles, close to Ullapool in Scotland, was badly damaged by illegal fishing practices like dredging. Dredging is when fishing boats drag heavy equipment along the ocean floor to catch fish, which can harm the environment.

**What happened** – Conservationists have found early signs that this damaged seabed is starting to recover. This positive change comes after the area was protected from harmful fishing activities, showing that the sea life and plants on the seabed can begin to heal when left undisturbed.

**Impact** – This recovery is important because it proves that protecting marine areas can help damaged ecosystems get better. It also supports the idea that stopping harmful fishing methods can save sea life. Groups like the Our Seas coalition, which includes scientists, activists, and some fishermen, are using this example to ask the Scottish government to protect more of Scotland’s coastal waters.

**What's next step** – The Our Seas coalition plans to present a petition to the Scottish Parliament, urging for stronger protections to cover at least 30% of Scotland’s inshore waters. Meanwhile, the government says it is committed to marine protection, but fishing leaders warn that banning certain boats might be too simple a solution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Early recovery of a damaged seabed near Scotland shows that marine protection efforts can help sea ecosystems heal when harmful fishing is stopped.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le fond marin protégé montre des signes précoces de guérison après des dégâts de pêche illégale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le fond marin près des Summer Isles, près d’Ullapool en Écosse, a été gravement endommagé par des pratiques de pêche illégales comme le dragage. Le dragage consiste à traîner du matériel lourd sur le fond de la mer pour attraper du poisson, ce qui peut abîmer l’environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des écologistes ont trouvé des signes précoces que ce fond marin endommagé commence à se rétablir. Ce changement positif arrive après que la zone a été protégée contre les activités de pêche nuisibles, montrant que la vie marine et les plantes du fond peuvent commencer à guérir quand elles ne sont pas dérangées.

**Impact** – Cette récupération est importante car elle prouve que protéger les zones marines peut aider les écosystèmes abîmés à se remettre. Cela soutient aussi l’idée que stopper les méthodes de pêche dangereuses peut sauver la vie marine. Des groupes comme la coalition Our Seas, qui rassemble scientifiques, militants et pêcheurs, utilisent cet exemple pour demander au gouvernement écossais de protéger plus de ses eaux côtières.

**Prochaine étape** – La coalition Our Seas prévoit de présenter une pétition au Parlement écossais pour demander une protection renforcée couvrant au moins 30 % des eaux côtières écossaises. Pendant ce temps, le gouvernement dit être engagé dans la protection marine, mais les responsables de la pêche avertissent qu’interdire certains bateaux pourrait être une solution trop simple.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La récupération précoce d’un fond marin endommagé en Écosse montre que les efforts de protection marine peuvent aider les écosystèmes marins à guérir quand la pêche nuisible est arrêtée.
articles.personality_title_es: El lecho marino protegido muestra señales tempranas de recuperación tras daños por pesca ilegal
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El lecho marino cerca de las Summer Isles, cerca de Ullapool en Escocia, fue gravemente dañado por prácticas ilegales de pesca como el dragado. El dragado es cuando los barcos de pesca arrastran equipos pesados por el fondo del mar para capturar peces, lo que puede dañar el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Los conservacionistas han encontrado señales tempranas de que este lecho marino dañado está comenzando a recuperarse. Este cambio positivo ocurre después de que la zona fue protegida de actividades de pesca dañinas, mostrando que la vida marina y las plantas en el fondo pueden comenzar a sanar cuando no son molestadas.

**Impacto** – Esta recuperación es importante porque demuestra que proteger áreas marinas puede ayudar a que los ecosistemas dañados mejoren. También apoya la idea de que detener métodos de pesca dañinos puede salvar la vida marina. Grupos como la coalición Our Seas, que incluye científicos, activistas y algunos pescadores, están usando este ejemplo para pedir al gobierno escocés que proteja más aguas costeras.

**Próximo paso** – La coalición Our Seas planea presentar una petición al Parlamento Escocés para pedir protecciones más fuertes que cubran al menos el 30 % de las aguas costeras de Escocia. Mientras tanto, el gobierno dice que está comprometido con la protección marina, pero los líderes pesqueros advierten que prohibir ciertos barcos podría ser una solución demasiado simple.

**Resumen en una frase** – La recuperación temprana de un lecho marino dañado cerca de Escocia muestra que los esfuerzos de protección marina pueden ayudar a los ecosistemas marinos a sanar cuando se detiene la pesca dañina.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant underwater seabed showing new coral and marine life gently regrowing where old fishing nets and trawl marks fade away, with silhouettes of peaceful fish swimming above a protected rocky coastline under soft natural light.

articles.title: Survey finds 'significantly more' ancient woodland
articles.summary: A wildlife trust says there are 50% more sites in Hertfordshire than previously recorded.
articles.content:
There is "significantly more" ancient woodland in Hertfordshire than previously recorded, new information confirmed.

The Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust (HMWT) said advancements in digital mapping and identification showed 50% more documented sites than originally compiled in an inventory of ancient woodland between 1981 and 1992.

It said the data submitted to Natural England would help to protect against future development and to improve management of sites for forestry and leisure.

The trust's records centre manager, Alex Waechter, said: "We have a chance to combat the losses and fragmentation of the past two centuries through the creation of new linking habitats and wildlife corridors."
articles.reasoning: The discovery of 50% more ancient woodland sites in Hertfordshire through improved mapping has significant positive implications for conservation, habitat protection, and biodiversity. This broadens the scope of environmental preservation efforts and provides actionable data to prevent future development harm, making it a meaningful advancement in environmental protection.
articles.personality_title: New survey reveals 50% more ancient woodlands in Hertfordshire
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ancient woodlands are forests that have existed for hundreds of years and are important for wildlife and nature. Previously, these woodlands were counted using older methods that missed some areas.

**What happened** – The Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust (HMWT) used new digital mapping tools to study Hertfordshire’s woodlands. They found 50% more ancient woodland sites than were recorded in surveys done between 1981 and 1992. This updated information was shared with Natural England, the government body responsible for protecting nature.

**Impact** – Finding many more ancient woodlands means these special places can get better protection from building or other changes that could harm them. It also helps people manage the forests for activities like walking and wildlife watching. This discovery gives a clearer picture of the natural environment and helps keep these important habitats safe.

**What's next step** – Using this new data, conservation groups and planners can work together to link woodlands with wildlife corridors. These corridors help animals move safely between areas, which is good for biodiversity. The hope is to reduce the damage caused by past development and support healthier forests in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Improved mapping has revealed many more ancient woodlands in Hertfordshire, strengthening efforts to protect and connect these valuable natural habitats.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle enquête révèle 50 % d'anciennes forêts en plus dans le Hertfordshire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les anciennes forêts sont des bois qui existent depuis des centaines d'années et sont importants pour la faune et la nature. Avant, ces forêts étaient comptées avec des méthodes anciennes qui en laissaient passer certaines.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust (HMWT) a utilisé de nouveaux outils de cartographie numérique pour étudier les forêts du Hertfordshire. Ils ont trouvé 50 % de sites d'anciennes forêts en plus que ceux enregistrés dans des enquêtes faites entre 1981 et 1992. Ces nouvelles données ont été partagées avec Natural England, l'organisme gouvernemental chargé de protéger la nature.

**Impact** – Trouver beaucoup plus d'anciennes forêts signifie que ces lieux spéciaux peuvent être mieux protégés contre la construction ou d'autres changements qui pourraient les abîmer. Cela aide aussi à mieux gérer les forêts pour des activités comme la promenade ou l'observation des animaux. Cette découverte donne une image plus claire de l'environnement naturel et aide à garder ces habitats importants en sécurité.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec ces nouvelles données, les groupes de conservation et les planificateurs peuvent travailler ensemble pour relier les forêts grâce à des corridors pour la faune. Ces corridors aident les animaux à se déplacer en sécurité entre les zones, ce qui est bon pour la biodiversité. L'espoir est de réduire les dégâts causés par les constructions passées et de soutenir des forêts plus saines à l'avenir.

**En une phrase** – Une meilleure cartographie a révélé beaucoup plus d'anciennes forêts dans le Hertfordshire, renforçant les efforts pour protéger et relier ces habitats naturels précieux.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva encuesta revela un 50 % más de bosques antiguos en Hertfordshire
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los bosques antiguos son áreas de árboles que han existido durante cientos de años y son importantes para la vida silvestre y la naturaleza. Antes, estos bosques se contaban con métodos antiguos que no detectaban todos los lugares.

**Qué pasó** – El Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust (HMWT) usó nuevas herramientas de mapeo digital para estudiar los bosques de Hertfordshire. Encontraron un 50 % más de sitios de bosques antiguos que los registrados en encuestas hechas entre 1981 y 1992. Esta información actualizada se compartió con Natural England, la organización gubernamental que protege la naturaleza.

**Impacto** – Encontrar muchos más bosques antiguos significa que estos lugares especiales pueden recibir mejor protección contra construcciones u otros cambios que podrían dañarlos. También ayuda a manejar mejor los bosques para actividades como caminar y observar animales. Este descubrimiento da una imagen más clara del ambiente natural y ayuda a mantener seguros estos hábitats importantes.

**Próximo paso** – Con estos nuevos datos, los grupos de conservación y planificadores pueden trabajar juntos para conectar los bosques mediante corredores para la vida silvestre. Estos corredores ayudan a los animales a moverse con seguridad entre áreas, lo que es bueno para la biodiversidad. La esperanza es reducir el daño causado por desarrollos anteriores y apoyar bosques más saludables en el futuro.

**En una frase** – El mapeo mejorado reveló muchos más bosques antiguos en Hertfordshire, fortaleciendo los esfuerzos para proteger y conectar estos valiosos hábitats naturales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sprawling ancient woodland with layered, textured tree trunks and dense undergrowth, overlaid with softly glowing, interconnected pathways symbolizing new wildlife corridors, set against a gentle sunrise in natural earthy tones.

articles.title: Outrage rescued an important ocean research program. Crucial ones remain at risk.
articles.content:
Just a few weeks ago, the Trump administration said it was going to pull hundreds of scientific instruments out of ocean waters near the Pacific Northwest, North Carolina, and the Irminger Sea, south of Greenland. It was part of the administration’s plan to roll back funding from a multimillion dollar research program dedicated to studying complex ocean and planetary dynamics, including climate change.

Then, last week, it put the brakes on that decision. The National Science Foundation said it would stop dismantling the sensors after a bipartisan group of senators pushed back and passed a measure blocking the agency from doing so. The federal agency also plans to put back the equipment it had already removed.

The attempt to dismantle the system, known as the Ocean Observatories Initiative, or OOI, was “supreme stupidity,” said Senator Jeff Merkley, a Democrat from Oregon who sponsored the measure along with Senator Lisa Murkowski, a Republican representing Alaska, in a statement. “We’ll keep fighting to ensure scientists, fishermen, and coastal communities can continue to utilize the critical data the OOI provides.”

This is not the first time that outrage and bipartisan support have saved climate-related research from the sweeping cuts enacted by the Trump administration. Sometimes to little fanfare, lawmakers have moved to preserve funding for scientific research at a number of agencies, as well as some environmental programs like Energy Star, which provides consumer rebates for purchasing energy-efficient appliances. Given their success, it’s likely that the administration’s anti-science agenda will continue meeting more backlash.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The Ocean Observatories Initiative had already been protected by lawmakers twice after the Trump administration proposed cutting the majority of its funding in 2025 and 2026 budgets. For now, the program, which began in 2016, is slated to continue operating for at least another decade.

But the initiative is not the only important ocean monitoring effort run by the United States. Researchers say other large cutting-edge ocean and climate research programs are facing a funding cliff with no plans in place to make sure they continue operating.

“We are seriously looking at the possibility of going dark,” said Lynne Talley, a professor at the Scripps Institution of Oceanography at the University of California, San Diego. Talley has been one of the scientists helping lead the Argo program, an international effort that has deployed thousands of underwater floats that bob up and down throughout ocean depths equipped with a variety of data-collecting sensors.

Since the first floats were deployed more than 25 years ago, the Argo program has provided scientists with an unprecedented ability to track changes in temperature, salinity, and heat throughout the oceans. Even when scientists use data from other ocean projects, they often combine or check it against data from the Argo program’s floats. A recent addition to the program expanded the network with biogeochemical sensors that measure oxygen, acidity, chlorophyll, and other indicators of ocean health.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

This helps scientists piece together the carbon cycle of oceans and how climate change is influencing it. “Those measurements have become essential to understanding the ocean,” Talley said.

The United States has deployed about half of the Argo program’s floats, which are battery powered and need to be replaced about every five years. But stagnant funding to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the agency leading the international initiative, means the pace of deployment has been too slow to maintain the program’s full coverage. The biogeochemical part of the network, backed by the National Science Foundation, ran out of funding last year, and will deploy its final floats this fall, with no plan in place to continue.

That means the Argo network could fail to remain fully operational in the future, even if other governments, such as the European Union’s, increase their contributions to it. “We have been the leaders in these ocean observations for many decades, and we are losing ground,” Talley said.

Researchers deploy an Argo float in 2009. Alicia Navidad, National Science Foundation / The Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation

In the next few years, two other U.S.-led initiatives studying one of the most pressing, and uncertain, climate threats lurking in the ocean could run out of federal funding, too. That threat is known as AMOC, the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation: a massive conveyor belt in the Atlantic Ocean that brings hot water to the north and cold water to the south. The system of currents is responsible for the climate many Europeans know today and is one of the reasons that Quebec experiences far more freezing days a year than London, despite sitting at a similar latitude.

Scientists think that climate change could cause it to slow down or collapse, which would result in dramatic and deadly weather changes in Europe and faster sea level rise along the eastern coast of North America. It has happened in the past, such as during the end of the last Ice Age, 12,000 years ago.

But for now, a scientific understanding of how likely that is to happen remains elusive. Strong year-to-year changes in ocean currents make it difficult to detect trends in how AMOC might be changing, and scientists remain divided on when and how soon the system might slow down. That makes studying it all the more important, said Susan Lozier, an oceanographer and the dean of the College of Sciences at Georgia Tech University.

Lozier co-leads OSNAP, one of two large research programs dedicated to studying the circulation system. These teams, which include researchers from the U.S. and six other countries, have placed dozens of moorings, outfitted with a variety of scientific instruments, across the North Atlantic, to capture changes in the water. OSNAP, in combination with the other program, RAPID, represent the best shot researchers currently have at getting to the bottom of the AMOC mystery, Lozier said.

But funding for U.S. participation in the projects depends on federal grants, which are on course to run out after next year, Lozier said. “We’re on pins and needles, waiting for what happens after that,” she said.

More than a year has passed since the program’s leaders submitted a proposal to the National Science Foundation for more funding. So far, Lozier said, they haven’t heard anything definitive. (The National Science Foundation did not respond to a request for comment.)

In the meantime, the Trump administration has slashed funding for science across the federal government, often targeting climate-focused research. Funding for geosciences like oceanography has fallen by more than half from last year. “We are really concerned about the possible continuation,” Lozier said. But it was heartening to hear about the reversal of the planned cuts to the OOI program, she said.

The Ocean Observatories Initiative also faced cutbacks in 2018 during Trump’s first term. That time, the downsizing was handled through a deliberate process that involved extensive input from scientists, engineers, and the National Science Board, said Jaime Palter, an oceanographer at the University of Rhode Island, in an email. The savings, she said, were redirected to support other ocean science work.



Commenting before learning that the funding to OOI was restored, Palter said the Trump administration’s recent actions were “fundamentally different” from last time. The speed of the planned dismantling of the initiative wouldn’t have given the scientific community time to adapt, she said.



Now that the decision has been reversed, it feels hopeful to know that people care about studying the ocean enough to push back on funding cuts, Palter said. But, she added, the uncertain future for programs like Argo make it “important not to let down our guard.”

“Destroying those capabilities can happen swiftly,” she said. “Rebuilding would be the work of a generation.”

Correction: An earlier version of this story misstated when the biogeochemical part of the Argo program ran out of funding.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant positive development where bipartisan political action successfully prevented the dismantling of a crucial ocean research program (Ocean Observatories Initiative) that contributes to understanding climate change and ocean health. This has broad societal and environmental significance, as ocean monitoring is vital for climate science and public safety. The story focuses on this single topic with detailed context about the program's importance, funding challenges, and the broader implications for climate research, making it a substantive and inspiring example of science and policy working together to protect environmental knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Bipartisan support saves key ocean research program from cuts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The U.S. government planned to remove important scientific instruments from the ocean as part of budget cuts. These instruments belong to the Ocean Observatories Initiative (OOI), a program that collects data to help scientists understand the ocean and climate change.

**What happened** – After public outrage and pressure from senators from both political parties, the National Science Foundation decided to stop removing the equipment. They will also reinstall instruments that had already been taken out. Senators Jeff Merkley and Lisa Murkowski led the effort to protect the program.

**Impact** – This decision means scientists, fishermen, and coastal communities can continue to use the data from the OOI, which helps track ocean conditions and climate changes. The program started in 2016 and is now secure for at least another ten years. However, other ocean research programs, like the Argo float network and projects studying the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), face funding problems and might stop working soon.

**What's next step** – Scientists and lawmakers will need to find ways to keep these other important programs running. The OOI’s saved funding shows that public and political support can protect ocean science. But without steady funding, other projects might “go dark,” meaning they could stop providing valuable information about the ocean and climate.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Strong bipartisan action has saved a vital ocean research program, highlighting the need to protect other climate science efforts from funding cuts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un soutien bipartite sauve un programme clé de recherche océanique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le gouvernement américain avait prévu de retirer des instruments scientifiques importants de l’océan dans le cadre de réductions budgétaires. Ces instruments font partie de l’Initiative d’Observatoires Océaniques (OOI), un programme qui collecte des données pour aider les scientifiques à comprendre l’océan et le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après une réaction publique et la pression de sénateurs des deux partis politiques, la Fondation Nationale pour la Science a décidé d’arrêter de retirer les équipements. Ils vont aussi réinstaller les instruments déjà enlevés. Les sénateurs Jeff Merkley et Lisa Murkowski ont mené cet effort pour protéger le programme.

**Impact** – Cette décision permet aux scientifiques, pêcheurs et communautés côtières de continuer à utiliser les données de l’OOI, qui aide à suivre les conditions océaniques et les changements climatiques. Le programme, lancé en 2016, est maintenant assuré pour au moins dix ans de plus. Cependant, d’autres programmes de recherche océanique, comme le réseau de flotteurs Argo et les projets étudiant la Circulation Méridionale de Renversement Atlantique (AMOC), ont des problèmes de financement et pourraient bientôt s’arrêter.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques et les législateurs devront trouver des moyens de maintenir ces autres programmes importants. Le financement sauvé de l’OOI montre que le soutien public et politique peut protéger la science océanique. Mais sans financement stable, d’autres projets pourraient « s’éteindre », c’est-à-dire arrêter de fournir des informations précieuses sur l’océan et le climat.

**En une phrase** – Une action bipartite forte a sauvé un programme vital de recherche océanique, soulignant le besoin de protéger d’autres efforts scientifiques contre les coupes budgétaires.
articles.personality_title_es: El apoyo bipartidista salva un programa clave de investigación oceánica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El gobierno de EE. UU. planeaba retirar instrumentos científicos importantes del océano como parte de recortes presupuestarios. Estos instrumentos pertenecen a la Iniciativa de Observatorios Oceánicos (OOI), un programa que recopila datos para ayudar a los científicos a entender el océano y el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Tras la protesta pública y la presión de senadores de ambos partidos, la Fundación Nacional de Ciencias decidió detener la remoción del equipo. También reinstalarán los instrumentos que ya habían sido retirados. Los senadores Jeff Merkley y Lisa Murkowski lideraron el esfuerzo para proteger el programa.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión significa que científicos, pescadores y comunidades costeras pueden seguir usando los datos del OOI, que ayudan a monitorear las condiciones oceánicas y los cambios climáticos. El programa comenzó en 2016 y ahora está asegurado por al menos diez años más. Sin embargo, otros programas de investigación oceánica, como la red de flotadores Argo y proyectos que estudian la Circulación Meridional de Vuelco del Atlántico (AMOC), enfrentan problemas de financiamiento y podrían dejar de funcionar pronto.

**Próximo paso** – Científicos y legisladores deberán encontrar formas de mantener estos otros programas importantes. El financiamiento salvado para el OOI muestra que el apoyo público y político puede proteger la ciencia oceánica. Pero sin fondos constantes, otros proyectos podrían “apagarse”, es decir, dejar de proveer información valiosa sobre el océano y el clima.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una fuerte acción bipartidista ha salvado un programa vital de investigación oceánica, destacando la necesidad de proteger otros esfuerzos científicos de los recortes presupuestarios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast ocean scene showing a network of glowing scientific instruments and floating sensors gently bobbing on and beneath the water’s surface near a vibrant coastline, with stylized ocean currents weaving through the scene like flowing ribbons, symbolizing ongoing research and the vital, interconnected efforts to monitor and protect the ocean’s health.
